title: Running Docker Containers on Your Linux Server
description: Install Docker and Docker Compose on Ubuntu, run your first container, deploy a WordPress stack with docker-compose, and set up Nginx as a reverse proxy in front of your containers.
prerequisites: A running Ubuntu 24.04 server with sudo access
Basic understanding of the Linux command line
Familiarity with YAML syntax is helpful but not required
learningObjectives: Install Docker Engine and Docker Compose on Ubuntu 24.04
Run and manage Docker containers from the command line
Write a docker-compose.yml file for a multi-container application
Use Docker volumes for persistent data
Configure Nginx as a reverse proxy for Docker containers

sponsorCta.text: You need a running Linux server for this exercise. Any Ubuntu server works, but we recommend DigitalOcean for quick setup.

steps.title: Install Docker Engine
steps.description: Install Docker from the official Docker repository. The Ubuntu default repository often has an older version, so we add Docker's own apt source.
commands: echo "deb [arch=$(dpkg --print-architecture) signed-by=/etc/apt/keyrings/docker.asc] https://download.docker.com/linux/ubuntu $(. /etc/os-release && echo "${VERSION_CODENAME}") stable" | sudo tee /etc/apt/sources.list.d/docker.list > /dev/null
sudo apt install -y docker-ce docker-ce-cli containerd.io docker-buildx-plugin docker-compose-plugin
steps.expectedOutput:
Reading package lists... Done
...
The following NEW packages will be installed:
  containerd.io docker-buildx-plugin docker-ce docker-ce-cli docker-compose-plugin
...
Setting up docker-ce ...
Created symlink /etc/systemd/system/multi-user.target.wants/docker.service ...
hints: These commands come directly from Docker's official installation guide for Ubuntu
Do not install docker.io from the Ubuntu repos; it is outdated
The docker-compose-plugin installs Compose V2, which uses 'docker compose' (without the hyphen)
If you get GPG errors, check that the keyring file was created correctly
validationCriteria: Docker is installed from the official repository
The docker service is running
docker-compose-plugin is installed

steps.title: Configure Docker and Verify the Installation
steps.description: Add your user to the docker group so you do not need sudo for every docker command, then run a test container.
steps.expectedOutput:
Docker version 27.x.x, build ...
Docker Compose version v2.x.x

Unable to find image 'hello-world:latest' locally
latest: Pulling from library/hello-world
...
Hello from Docker!
This message shows that your installation appears to be working correctly.
...
hints: After adding your user to the docker group, you need to log out and back in, or use newgrp
If 'docker run hello-world' fails with a permission error, you probably need to re-login
docker compose (without hyphen) is the V2 syntax; docker-compose (with hyphen) is the legacy V1
validationCriteria: docker --version shows the installed version
docker compose version works
hello-world container runs successfully without sudo

steps.title: Run and Manage Containers
steps.description: Practice the basic Docker commands by running an Nginx container, checking logs, and cleaning up.
steps.expectedOutput:
Unable to find image 'nginx:latest' locally
...
Status: Downloaded newer image for nginx:latest
abc123def456...

CONTAINER ID   IMAGE         COMMAND                  CREATED         STATUS         PORTS                  NAMES
abc123def456   nginx:latest  "/docker-entrypoint..."   5 seconds ago   Up 4 seconds   0.0.0.0:8080->80/tcp   test-nginx

<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>
<head>
<title>Welcome to nginx!</title>
...

test-nginx
test-nginx
hints: -d runs the container in detached (background) mode
--name gives the container a memorable name instead of a random one
-p 8080:80 maps port 8080 on the host to port 80 inside the container
docker ps shows only running containers; use docker ps -a to see stopped ones too
Always stop and remove test containers when you are done
validationCriteria: The Nginx container starts and serves the default page on port 8080
docker ps shows the running container
You can view container logs
The container is stopped and removed cleanly

steps.title: Deploy WordPress with Docker Compose
steps.description: Create a docker-compose.yml file that runs WordPress with a MySQL database. This is a realistic multi-container setup that you would actually use in production.
steps.codeExample:
# ~/wordpress/docker-compose.yml
services:
  db:
    image: mysql:8.0
    restart: unless-stopped
    environment:
      MYSQL_ROOT_PASSWORD: rootpassword123
      MYSQL_DATABASE: wordpress
      MYSQL_USER: wpuser
      MYSQL_PASSWORD: wppassword123
    volumes:
      - db_data:/var/lib/mysql
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD", "mysqladmin", "ping", "-h", "localhost"]
      interval: 10s
      timeout: 5s
      retries: 5

  wordpress:
    image: wordpress:latest
    restart: unless-stopped
    depends_on:
      db:
        condition: service_healthy
    environment:
      WORDPRESS_DB_HOST: db:3306
      WORDPRESS_DB_USER: wpuser
      WORDPRESS_DB_PASSWORD: wppassword123
      WORDPRESS_DB_NAME: wordpress
    ports:
      - "8080:80"
    volumes:
      - wp_data:/var/www/html

volumes:
  db_data:
  wp_data:
steps.expectedOutput: Create the file, then proceed to the next step to start the stack.
hints: Use strong, unique passwords in a real deployment; these are just examples
Named volumes (db_data, wp_data) persist data even if the container is removed
depends_on with condition: service_healthy ensures MySQL is ready before WordPress starts
Create the file with: nano ~/wordpress/docker-compose.yml
validationCriteria: docker-compose.yml is created in ~/wordpress/
The file defines two services: db and wordpress
Named volumes are defined for data persistence
Environment variables are set for database connection

steps.title: Start the WordPress Stack
steps.description: Use docker compose to bring up the WordPress and MySQL containers. Docker will pull the images, create volumes, and start everything.
steps.expectedOutput:
[+] Running 3/3
 ✔ Network wordpress_default  Created
 ✔ Container wordpress-db-1   Started
 ✔ Container wordpress-wordpress-1  Started

NAME                    IMAGE              COMMAND                  SERVICE     CREATED          STATUS                    PORTS
wordpress-db-1          mysql:8.0          "docker-entrypoint..."   db          30 seconds ago   Up 29 seconds (healthy)   3306/tcp, 33060/tcp
wordpress-wordpress-1   wordpress:latest   "docker-entrypoint..."   wordpress   30 seconds ago   Up 20 seconds             0.0.0.0:8080->80/tcp
hints: docker compose up -d starts all services in the background
docker compose logs -f follows the logs in real time; press Ctrl+C to stop
Wait for the db service to show (healthy) before testing WordPress
Visit http://YOUR_SERVER_IP:8080 to see the WordPress installation wizard
validationCriteria: Both containers are running
The db container shows a healthy status
WordPress is accessible on port 8080
No errors in the container logs

steps.title: Manage the Docker Compose Stack
steps.description: Learn the essential docker compose commands for day-to-day management: stopping, starting, viewing resource usage, and accessing container shells.
commands: docker compose exec db mysql -u wpuser -pwppassword123 -e 'SHOW DATABASES;'
steps.expectedOutput:
NAME                    IMAGE              ... STATUS
wordpress-db-1          mysql:8.0          ... Up (healthy)
wordpress-wordpress-1   wordpress:latest   ... Up

CONTAINER ID   NAME                    CPU %   MEM USAGE / LIMIT     MEM %   NET I/O       BLOCK I/O
abc123         wordpress-db-1          0.30%   180MiB / 976MiB       18.4%   1.2kB / 0B    25MB / 1MB
def456         wordpress-wordpress-1   0.05%   45MiB / 976MiB        4.6%    800B / 0B     12MB / 0B

/var/www/html/wp-config.php

+--------------------+
| Database           |
+--------------------+
| information_schema |
| wordpress          |
+--------------------+

DRIVER    VOLUME NAME
local     wordpress_db_data
local     wordpress_wp_data
hints: docker stats shows real-time resource usage for all containers
--no-stream prints stats once instead of updating continuously
docker compose exec runs a command inside a running container
Use docker compose stop to pause services and docker compose start to resume them
docker compose down stops and removes containers but keeps volumes; add -v to also remove volumes
validationCriteria: You can check container status and resource usage
You can execute commands inside running containers
You can access the MySQL database from the db container
You can list Docker volumes

steps.title: Set Up Nginx as a Reverse Proxy
steps.description: Instead of exposing WordPress directly on port 8080, put Nginx in front of it as a reverse proxy. This lets you use your domain name, add SSL later, and serve multiple apps from one server.
steps.expectedOutput:
nginx: the configuration file /etc/nginx/nginx.conf syntax is ok
nginx: configuration file /etc/nginx/nginx.conf test is successful
hints: proxy_pass forwards all requests to the WordPress container on port 8080
The proxy_set_header lines pass the real client IP and protocol to WordPress
client_max_body_size 64M allows uploading larger files through WordPress
After this, update your docker-compose.yml to bind port 8080 to 127.0.0.1 only: '127.0.0.1:8080:80'
You can add SSL with Certbot just like in exercise 3
validationCriteria: Nginx config test passes
WordPress is accessible through Nginx on port 80
The proxy headers are correctly forwarded
The default Nginx site is removed

steps.title: Lock Down the Direct Docker Port
steps.description: Now that Nginx proxies traffic to WordPress, stop exposing port 8080 to the public internet. Update docker-compose.yml to bind only to localhost.
steps.expectedOutput:
[+] Running 2/2
 ✔ Container wordpress-db-1          Running
 ✔ Container wordpress-wordpress-1   Started

NAME                    ... PORTS
wordpress-db-1          ... 3306/tcp, 33060/tcp
wordpress-wordpress-1   ... 127.0.0.1:8080->80/tcp

HTTP/1.1 200 OK
...

HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Server: nginx
...
hints: Binding to 127.0.0.1 means only local connections are accepted on port 8080
External visitors can only reach WordPress through Nginx on port 80
docker compose up -d recreates only the containers that changed
This is a security best practice for any service behind a reverse proxy
validationCriteria: Port 8080 is bound to 127.0.0.1 only
WordPress is not accessible on port 8080 from outside the server
WordPress is accessible through Nginx on port 80
Both containers are running

completionCriteria: Docker and Docker Compose are installed and working
You can run, manage, and remove Docker containers
A WordPress + MySQL stack is running with docker compose
Data persists in named Docker volumes
Nginx reverse proxy is configured in front of the Docker containers
Direct container ports are not exposed to the public internet

troubleshooting.issue: Docker compose up fails with 'port is already allocated'
troubleshooting.solution: Another process is using port 8080. Find it with 'sudo ss -tlnp | grep 8080' and stop it, or change the port mapping in docker-compose.yml to a different port like 8081:80.

troubleshooting.issue: WordPress shows 'Error establishing a database connection'
troubleshooting.solution: The MySQL container might not be ready yet. Check its health status with 'docker compose ps'. Verify the database credentials in docker-compose.yml match between the db and wordpress services. Check MySQL logs with 'docker compose logs db'.

The upstream (WordPress container) is not responding. Verify the container is running with 'docker compose ps'. Check that the port in proxy_pass matches the mapped port. Test the upstream directly: 'curl http://127.0.0.1:8080'.
